content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 17, 1992

Joseph M. Morales
Hahn & Hahn
301 East Colorado Blvd., Ninth Floor
Pasadena, CA 91101-1977

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-92-638

Dear Mr. Morales:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because you have not
identified the person on whose behalf you are requesting this
advice, we can only provide the following informal response to
your question pursuant to Regulation 18329(c)(3).2/

QUESTION

Will a group of concerned citizens incur a reporting
obligation under the Act if the group receives funds for the
purpose of underwriting the costs of conducting a city special
election?

CONCLUSION

If the group receives $1,000 or more in a calendar year to
conduct the election, it will qualify as a committee under the Act
and must file campaign disclosure reports.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95812 ● (916) 322-5660

Joseph Morales
Our File No. I-92-638
Page 2


FACTS

A group of concerned citizens wants to raise funds for the
purpose of underwriting the cost of holding a special city
election. The special election ballot will contain a measure to
enact a special tax for the funding of a single city service which
has been cut from the city's general fund.


ANALYSIS

The Act imposes filing requirements on persons who receive
$1,000 or more in a calendar for "political purposes."  Section
82013 defines a "committee" in relevant part as a person or
combination of persons who directly or indirectly receives
"contributions" totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
(Section 82013(a).)

"Contribution" is defined in the Act as any payment,
forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that
full and adequate consideration is received (unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes).  (Section 82015.)

Regulation 18215 defines "a payment for political purposes,"
in relevant part, as a payment:

     (1)  For the purpose of influencing or attempting
     to influence the action of the voters for or
     against the nomination or election of a candidate or
     candidates, or the qualification or passage of any
     measure....

The Commission has previously issued written advice on the
issue of a person making payments for the costs of conducting an
election.  (See Winkler Advice Letter, No. A-87-023, and McNeil Advice
Letter, No. A-92-46, copies enclosed.)  The Winkler and McNeil
letters state that payments made by a person for the cost of
conducting an election are expenditures for political purposes and
are required to be reported.  If the group receives $1,000 or more
in a calendar year for the purpose of making such expenditures, it
will be required to file a Statement of Organization with the
Secretary of State and file periodic recipient committee campaign
statements disclosing its contributions and expenditures.

Joseph M. Morales
Our File No. I-92-638
Page 3


The necessary campaign disclosure forms and manual may be
obtained from the city clerk's office.

Please do not hesitate to contact me at 916/322-5662 if you
have additional questions.

Sincerely,

Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures
